attrs.alt: Risk detection flow from signal input through detection engine to automated actions, and risk state lifecycle

content.text: Microsoft Entra ID Protection analyzes billions of sign-in events to build per-user behavioral baselines and detect anomalous activity. When a sign-in or account-level behavior matches a known attack pattern or deviates significantly from the baseline, Identity Protection creates a risk detection and assigns a risk level (High, Medium, Low) to the associated sign-in or user.

Detections fall into three categories: sign-in risk (suspicious characteristics of an individual sign-in), user risk (account-level indicators of compromise), and workload identity risk (suspicious service principal behavior). Each category feeds into a risk level that CA policies can use as a condition.
Real-time detections evaluate during the sign-in and can block or require MFA before the token is issued.
Offline detections run after the sign-in using aggregated signals and ML models — they may update a risk level hours later.
A single sign-in can trigger multiple detection types simultaneously (e.g., anonymous IP + unfamiliar sign-in properties).
Sign-in from Tor exit node, anonymous VPN, or known proxy IP
Sign-ins from two geographically distant locations faster than physically possible
Unusual browser, OS, ASN, or location compared to user's historical baseline
Sign-in IP associated with active botnet command-and-control
Inbox rules created to forward or delete emails — common post-compromise behavior
Same password tried across many accounts; detected cross-account by ML
Duplicate of atypical travel; Microsoft uses both terms for related detections
Token obtained via AiTM phishing proxy; detected via IP/token fingerprint mismatch
Automated sign-in activity characteristic of credential stuffing or bot traffic
Unusually high volume of file accesses — indicative of data exfiltration

AiTM (Adversary-in-the-middle) phishing is one of the highest-impact detections. An attacker proxies the entire sign-in including MFA completion, then reuses the stolen session token. Detection is based on IP mismatch between the sign-in event and the token use event — traditional MFA does NOT prevent this. Only phishing-resistant methods (FIDO2, WHfB) block AiTM attacks.
Credentials found in dark web breach dumps matched against Entra ID accounts
Microsoft internal threat intelligence signals — exact source not disclosed
Unusual pattern of resource access or configuration changes relative to baseline
Service principal or user making unusual Graph API calls (e.g., bulk user enumeration)
User flags a sign-in notification as 'not me'; triggers risk elevation
Reading Risk Detections via Graph API

The riskDetections and riskyUsers endpoints expose the full detection data. Use these to build investigation workflows, export to SIEM, or automate remediation responses beyond what CA policies provide.

# List all active risk detections (last 30 days by default)
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'IdentityRiskEvent.Read.All', 'IdentityRiskyUser.Read.All'

$detections = Get-MgRiskDetection -Top 50 -Filter "riskState eq 'atRisk'"
foreach ($d in $detections) {
    [PSCustomObject]@{
        Id               = $d.Id
        DetectionType    = $d.RiskEventType
        RiskLevel        = $d.RiskLevel
        RiskState        = $d.RiskState
        User             = $d.UserPrincipalName
        Timestamp        = $d.ActivityDateTime
        IPAddress        = $d.IpAddress
        Location         = $d.Location.City + ', ' + $d.Location.CountryOrRegion
        DetectionTiming  = $d.DetectionTimingType  # realtime | offline | notDefined
    }
} | Format-Table -AutoSize

# Filter to high-risk sign-in detections only
$highRisk = Get-MgRiskDetection -Filter "riskLevel eq 'high' and riskType eq 'signIn'"

The riskyUsers endpoint shows accounts with elevated user risk. Administrators can confirm compromise (triggering forced password reset and revocation of all sessions) or dismiss risk (marking it as a false positive). CA policies also auto-remediate user risk when a user completes SSPR.

# List all risky users (users with elevated user risk level)
$riskyUsers = Get-MgRiskyUser -Filter "riskLevel eq 'high' or riskLevel eq 'medium'"
foreach ($u in $riskyUsers) {
    [PSCustomObject]@{
        User         = $u.UserPrincipalName
        RiskLevel    = $u.RiskLevel
        RiskState    = $u.RiskState
        RiskDetail   = $u.RiskDetail
        LastUpdated  = $u.RiskLastUpdatedDateTime
    }
} | Format-Table

# Get detection history for a specific risky user
$history = Get-MgRiskyUserHistory -RiskyUserId '<user-object-id>'
foreach ($h in $history) {
    Write-Host "$($h.ActivityDateTime): $($h.RiskState) — $($h.RiskEventTypes)"
}

# Confirm a user as compromised (marks risk state = confirmedCompromised)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskyUsers/confirmCompromised' \
    -Body (@{ userIds = @('<user-object-id>') } | ConvertTo-Json)

# Dismiss risk for a user (FP — confirmed safe)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskyUsers/dismiss' \
    -Body (@{ userIds = @('<user-object-id>') } | ConvertTo-Json)

With Workload ID Premium, Identity Protection also monitors service principals and managed identities. Detections include anomalous credential usage, suspicious token issuance patterns, and threats from Microsoft's intelligence feed. Workload identity risk does not integrate with CA policies — remediation is manual or via automation.

# List risky service principals (Workload Identity Premium required)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskyServicePrincipals' \
    -OutputType PSObject | Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
    Select-Object displayName, riskLevel, riskState, riskLastUpdatedDateTime

# List service principal risk detections
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/servicePrincipalRiskDetections' \
    -OutputType PSObject | Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
    Select-Object servicePrincipalDisplayName, riskEventType, riskLevel, activityDateTime

# Dismiss a risky service principal
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskyServicePrincipals/dismiss' \
    -Body (@{ servicePrincipalIds = @('<sp-object-id>') } | ConvertTo-Json)

Identity Protection streams risk events to the AADUserRiskEvents and AADRiskyUsers tables in Log Analytics. Use these for real-time alerting, trend analysis, and correlation across multiple compromised accounts.

// KQL: high-risk detections by type in the last 7 days
AADRiskyUsers
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where RiskLevel == 'high'
| summarize Count = count() by RiskEventTypes, RiskState
| order by Count desc

// KQL: AiTM detections — critical: indicates active phishing campaign
AADUserRiskEvents
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(24h)
| where RiskEventType == 'investigationsThreatIntelligence'
     or RiskEventType == 'mcasSuspiciousInboxManipulationRules'
     or RiskEventType == 'aiTMPhishing'
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, RiskLevel, IpAddress, Location, RiskEventType
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// KQL: correlation — same IP appearing in multiple risk detections
AADUserRiskEvents
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| summarize Users = dcount(UserPrincipalName), Events = count() by IpAddress, RiskEventType
| where Users > 1
| order by Users desc
Risk-based CA policies (sign-in and user risk)
All Entra ID P2 features via bundle
Microsoft 365 E5, EMS E5, or Entra ID P2 standalone

Every risk detection and user risk event progresses through a defined set of states. Understanding the lifecycle is essential for building accurate SIEM dashboards and automated remediation workflows — different states indicate whether action has been taken and what kind.
Active risk — not yet remediated or dismissed
Set by Identity Protection when a detection is created
User completed a remediation action (password change via SSPR, or MFA satisfied a risk policy)
Automatic when CA user risk policy triggers SSPR; manual via portal
Admin reviewed and determined the detection was a false positive
Admin confirms via portal or API: POST /riskyUsers/dismiss
Admin confirmed the account is compromised
Admin sets via portal or POST /riskyUsers/confirmCompromised
Admin reviewed and determined the sign-in was legitimate
Admin sets on sign-in risk event; does NOT affect user risk level
'remediated' means Identity Protection considers the risk resolved — either the user completed SSPR through a CA user-risk policy, or an admin manually set it.
'confirmedCompromised' immediately triggers a forced sign-out and disables future sign-ins until an admin re-enables the account — use it only when compromise is certain.
'dismissed' does NOT revoke sessions or require password change — it only marks the event as a false positive for reporting purposes.
Risk states feed back into CA policy evaluation: a dismissed user returns to risk level 'none'; a confirmedCompromised user stays at 'high' until admin action.

The primary automation for risk response is through CA policies that reference signInRiskLevels and userRiskLevels as conditions. This creates a closed loop: detection raises risk → CA policy enforces remediation (MFA or SSPR) → user satisfies the control → risk is auto-remediated.

The three-tier risk policy set (block High sign-in risk, require MFA for Medium, require password change for High user risk) is Microsoft's recommended starting configuration for Identity Protection. Deploy all three in Report-Only mode for 48 hours before enabling to assess impact.

For use cases that require immediate response (SOC alerting, automated account suspension), subscribe to the riskDetections change feed via Microsoft Graph webhooks. Your endpoint receives a POST notification within seconds of a new detection being created.

# Subscribe to risk detection events via Microsoft Graph webhook
# This lets your SIEM or automation platform receive detections in near-real-time
$body = @{
    ChangeType = 'created'
    NotificationUrl = 'https://your-endpoint.example.com/webhooks/risk'
    Resource = 'identityProtection/riskDetections'
    ExpirationDateTime = '2026-08-01T00:00:00Z'
    ClientState = 'secretToken123'  # validate in your endpoint handler
} | ConvertTo-Json

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/subscriptions' \
    -Body $body -ContentType 'application/json'

# Renew subscription before expiry (max lifetime is 4230 minutes ~ 3 days for risk events)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/subscriptions/<subscription-id>' \
    -Body (@{ ExpirationDateTime = '2026-08-04T00:00:00Z' } | ConvertTo-Json) \
    -ContentType 'application/json'

During a security incident, you may need to investigate multiple high-risk users simultaneously. This script enumerates all high-risk accounts and pulls their recent detections and sign-in history to accelerate triage.

Identity Protection diagnostic logs must be explicitly enabled in Entra diagnostic settings. Once streaming, four tables become available in Log Analytics: AADRiskyUsers, AADUserRiskEvents, AADRiskyServicePrincipals, and AADServicePrincipalRiskEvents.

Not every detection represents a real threat. Identity Protection prioritizes recall (catching real attacks) over precision, which means some legitimate sign-ins are flagged. Use the table below to recognize common FP patterns and respond appropriately.
Corporate VPN with anonymizing exit node
Dismiss; add VPN egress IPs to a named location
User flew to another office; both sign-ins are legitimate
Dismiss the sign-in risk; consider excluding frequent travelers from atypical travel policy
New device or browser after hardware refresh
Dismiss; user should register the new device
Automated testing tool using the same password against test accounts
Dismiss for test accounts; isolate test infrastructure to separate tenant
Credential found in breach dump pre-dates Entra ID (old password already changed)
Dismiss if user's current password is different; force password change if uncertain
False positive very rare; Microsoft has high confidence before flagging
Investigate thoroughly before dismissing; treat as confirmed compromise until disproven
Identity Protection detects risky sign-ins and user-level compromise using ML models, behavior baselines, and Microsoft threat intelligence.
Sign-in risk covers individual authentication events (anonymous IP, AiTM, atypical travel); user risk covers account-level indicators (leaked credentials, anomalous activity).
Real-time detections block or challenge during sign-in; offline detections update risk levels hours later with more accurate analysis.
AiTM phishing is the most impactful detection — traditional MFA does not prevent it; phishing-resistant methods (FIDO2, WHfB) do.
Graph API endpoints (riskDetections, riskyUsers, riskyServicePrincipals) expose detection data for SIEM integration and automated remediation.
Workload identity risk (service principals) requires Workload ID Premium and does not integrate with CA risk conditions.
